concepts.title: Arithmetic Mean
concepts.conceptBody:
Arithmetic Mean (AM) 

When three quantities are in $\text{AP}$, then the middle one is a arithmetic mean of other two.

If $\text{a}$ and $\text{b}$ are two numbers and $\text{A}$ is arithmetic mean of $\text{a}$ and $\text{b}$, then $\text{a},\text{A},\text{b}$ are in AP.

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}-\text{a}=\text{b}-\text{A}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}=\frac{\text{a}+\text{b}}{2}$

So if $\text{a},\text{b},\text{c}$ are 3 numbers in AP then $\text{b}$ is the arithmetic mean of a & c.

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}-\text{a}=\text{c}-\text{b}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}=\frac{\text{a}+\text{c}}{2}$

concepts.title: Inserting n-number of Arithmetic Means between two given numbers
concepts.conceptBody:
Inserting n arithmetic means between two number 

${{\text{A}}_{1}}, {{\text{A}}_{2}}, {{\text{A}}_{3}},\ldots \ldots .{{\text{A}}_{\text{n}}}$ are called $\text{n}$ arithmetic means between two numbers $\text{a}$ and $\text{b}$, if the series a, ${{A}_{1}},{{A}_{2}},{{A}_{3}},{{A}_{4}},\ldots ..,{{A}_{n}},b$ is an AP.

For this AP, first term is a, number of terms is $\left( n+2 \right)$, & the last term $=b={{T}_{n+2}}$. Let $d$ be the common difference of this AP, then

$\begin{array}{l} {} &  \Rightarrow  {{\text{T}}_{\text{n}+2}}=\text{b}=\text{a}+\left\{ \left( \text{n}+2 \right)-1 \right\}\text{d} \\ {} &  \Rightarrow  \text{ b}=\text{a}+\left( \text{n}+1 \right)\text{d} \\ {} &  \Rightarrow  \text{ d}=\frac{\text{b}-\text{a}}{\text{n}+1} \\ {} &  \Rightarrow {{A}_{1}}=a+d=a+\frac{b-a}{n+1} \text{ and } {{A}_{2}}=a+2d=a+2\frac{b-a}{n+1} \\ \end{array}$

$\text{ In } \text{ general } {{\text{k}}^{\text{th } }} \text{ arithmetic } \text{ mean } \text{ is } {{\text{A}}_{\text{k}}}=\text{a}+\text{kd}=\text{a}+\text{k}\frac{\text{b}-\text{a}}{\text{n}+1}$

concepts.title: Sum of all Arithmetic Means inserted between two numbers
concepts.conceptBody:
Important Point to remember

Sum of all AM's inserted between two numbers a & b is $n\left( \frac{a+b}{2} \right)$ i.e. $\sum _{i=1}^{n}{{A}_{i}}=n\left( \frac{a+b}{2} \right)$

Proof : We know ${{A}_{i}}=a+i\frac{\left( b-a \right)}{n+1}$

So $\sum_{i=1}^n A_i=\sum_{i=1}^n a+\sum_{i=1}^n i \frac{(b-a)}{n+1}$

$=a \sum_{i=1}^n 1+\left(\frac{b-a}{n+a}\right) \sum_{i=1}^n i$

$=a(n)+\left(\frac{b-a}{n+1}\right)(1+2+3 \ldots n)$

$=\mathrm{a}(\mathrm{n})+\left(\frac{\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{n}+1}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2}\right)$

$=a n=\frac{(b-a) n}{2}$

$=\mathrm{n}\left(\mathrm{a}+\frac{\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{a}}{2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b})}{2}$

concepts.title: Solved Example based on property of Arithmetic Mean
concepts.conceptBody:
Solved Example based on property of Arithmetic Mean

Q) The sum of 2 numbers is $\frac{13}{6}$. An even number of arithmetic means are inserted between them and their sum exceeds their number by 1 . Find the number of means inserted. 

Solution : 

Let the 2 numbers be a $\And b$. then, $a+b=\frac{13}{6}$ (1)

Suppose ${{A}_{1}},{{A}_{2}}\ldots .{{A}_{2N}}$ be $2n$ arithmetic means inserted between a & $b$.

Then ${{\text{A}}_{1}}+{{\text{A}}_{2}}+\ldots {{\text{A}}_{2\text{n}}}=2\text{n}\left( \frac{\text{a}+\text{b}}{2} \right)$

$\left( \because \right.$ We know that sum of AM's is $\left. \frac{n}{2}\left( a+b \right) \right)$ putting value from (i)

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{A}}_{1}}+{{\text{A}}_{2}}\ldots {{\text{A}}_{2\text{n}}}=\text{n}\left( \frac{13}{6} \right)$ (2)

Also it is given in the question that

${{\text{A}}_{1}}+{{\text{A}}_{2}}\ldots .{{\text{A}}_{2\text{n}}}=2\text{n}+1$ (3)

equating (ii) & (iii)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{n}\left( \frac{13}{6} \right)=2\text{n}+1 \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & 13\text{n}=12\text{n}+6 \\ {} & \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ n}=6 \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore 12$ means are inserted between a & b.

concepts.title: Geometric Mean
concepts.conceptBody:
Geometric Means (G.M.) 

If ${{G}_{1}},{{G}_{2}},{{G}_{3}},{{G}_{4}}\ldots \ldots .{{G}_{n}}$ are GM's between $a$ and $b$ then $a,G1,{{G}_{2}},\ldots \ldots .{{G}_{n}}$, $b$ are in G.P.

Now $\text{b}$ is ${{\text{t}}_{\text{n}+2}}$ so $\text{b}=a{{r}^{\text{n}+1}}$

Or $r={{\left( \frac{b}{a} \right)}^{\frac{1}{n+1}}}$

and thus ${{G}_{1}}=a{{\left( \frac{b}{a} \right)}^{\frac{1}{n+1}}}$

concepts.title: Properties of Geometric Mean
concepts.conceptBody:
Property of GM 

(1) The product of $n$ G.M's between $a$ and $b$ is equal to ${{n}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ power of one G.M. between a and b. i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{G}_{1}},{{G}_{2}},\ldots \ldots {{G}_{n}}={{(\sqrt{ab})}^{n}}$

i.e. $\mathrm{G}_1, \mathrm{G}_2, \ldots \ldots \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{n}}=(\sqrt{\mathrm{ab}})^{\mathrm{n}}$

$\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{a}\left(\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}}\right)^{\frac{\mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{n}+1}}$

$\mathrm{G}_1, \mathrm{G}_2, \ldots . . \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{n}} \sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}}\right)^{\frac{\mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{n}+1}}$

$=a^n\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)^{\frac{\Sigma i}{n+1}}$

$=a^n\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)^{\frac{n(b+1)}{2(n+1)}}$

$=(\mathrm{ab})^{\frac{\mathrm{n}}{2}}=(\sqrt{\mathrm{ab}})^{\mathrm{n}}$

(2) If ${{\text{a}}_{1}},{{\text{a}}_{2}},\ldots \ldots \ldots {{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}$ are $\text{n}$ non-zero numbers then their G.M is given by

$\text{G}={{\left( {{\text{a}}_{1}},{{\text{a}}_{2}}\ldots \ldots {{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}} \right)}^{\frac{1}{\text{n}}}}$

Note: You can check the analogy of formulas between Arithmetic Progression & Geometric progression.

concepts.title: Solved Problems based on Properties of Geometric Mean
concepts.conceptBody:
Solved examples

Q) If we insert odd number $\left( 2n+1 \right)$ G.M.'s between 4 and 2916 then find the value of ${{(n+1)}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ G.M.? 

Solution : 

Now $4,{{G}_{1}},{{G}_{2}},{{G}_{3}}\ldots \ldots \ldots {{G}_{n+1}},\ldots \ldots .{{G}_{2n}},{{G}_{2n+1}},2916$ are in G.P. So ${{G}_{n+1}}$ will be the middle mean of $\left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)$ odd means and so it will equidistant from ${{1}^{\text{st }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ and last term. So, $4,{{\text{G}}_{\text{n}+1}},2916$ are also in GP.

And thus,

$\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{n}+1}=(4.2916)^{\frac{1}{2}}$

$=(4 \times 9 \times 324)^{\frac{1}{2}}$

$=(4 \times 9 \times 4 \times 81)^{\frac{1}{2}}$

$=\left(\begin{array}{llll}2 & 3 & 2 & 9\end{array}\right)$

$=108$

concepts.title: Properties of Harmonic Mean
concepts.conceptBody:
Harmonic Means (H.M.)

If ${{\text{H}}_{1}},{{\text{H}}_{2}},{{\text{H}}_{3}}\ldots \ldots .{{\text{H}}_{\text{n}}}$ be $\text{n}$ H.M.'s between a and $\text{b}$ then $\text{a},{{\text{H}}_{1}},{{\text{H}}_{2}},{{\text{H}}_{3}}\ldots \ldots ..{{\text{H}}_{\text{n}}}$, b is a H.P.

This means $\frac{1}{\text{a}},\frac{1}{\text{H}},\frac{1}{{{\text{H}}_{2}}}\ldots \ldots \frac{1}{{{\text{H}}_{\text{n}}}},\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}}$ is a A.P.

And hence $\frac{1}{{{\text{H}}_{\text{i}}}}=\frac{1}{\text{a}}+\frac{\text{i}\left( \text{a}-\text{b} \right)}{\left( \text{n}+1 \right)\text{ab}}$

Note : If ${{a}_{1}},{{a}_{2}},{{a}_{3}}\ldots ....$. an are $n$ non-zero numbers then H.M. of these number is given by

$H=\frac{n}{\frac{1}{{{a}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{{{a}_{2}}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots +\frac{1}{{{a}_{n}}}}$

$\text{a},\text{b},\text{c}$ are in H.P. so, $\frac{1}{\text{a}},\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}},\frac{1}{\text{c}}$ are in $\text{AP}$

And $\frac{2}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}}=\frac{1}{\text{a}}+\frac{1}{\text{c}}\Rightarrow \frac{2\text{ac}}{\text{a}+\text{c}}=\text{b}$

concepts.title: Relation between Arithmetic ,Geometric and Harmonic Mean
concepts.conceptBody:
Some Important Theorems 

Let $\text{A},\text{G},\text{H}$ be the $\text{AM},\text{GM},\text{HM}$ respectively between two positive unequal quantities. First of all let us prove $A>G$

$\text{A}=\frac{\text{x}+\text{y}}{2}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ G}=\sqrt{\text{xy}}$

So to prove $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{x}+\text{y}}{2}>\sqrt{\text{xy}}$

Now $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{(\sqrt{\text{x}}-\sqrt{\text{y}})}^{2}}>0(\therefore \text{x},\text{y}$ are positive $)$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}+\text{y}-2\sqrt{\text{xy}}>0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{x}+\text{y}}{2}>\sqrt{\text{xy}}$

Hence A > G (i)

Now let us prove $\text{G}>\text{H}$

Again $H=\frac{2xy}{x+y}$

Also ${{(\sqrt{\text{x}}-\text{y})}^{2}}>0$

$\Rightarrow $ $x+y>2\sqrt{xy}$

$\Rightarrow $ $1>\frac{2\sqrt{xy}}{x+y}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ G}>\text{H}$ (ii)

Combining (1) and (2) we get $\mathbf{A}>\mathbf{G}>\mathbf{H}$

Now we can prove :

$G^2$ = AH 

Let $\text{x},\text{y}$ be two numbers.

So, $A=\frac{x+y}{2},G=\sqrt{xy},H=\frac{2xy}{x+y}$

Hence $A=\frac{x+y}{2}, G=\sqrt{x y}, H=\frac{2 x y}{x+y}$

$\mathrm{AH}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}{2}\right)\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{xy}}{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}\right)$

$=x y$

$=\mathrm{G}^2$

concepts.title: Some Solved Problems based on the inequality existing between Arithmetic,Geometric and Harmonic Progression
concepts.conceptBody:
Solved examples

Q) If $a,b,c,d$ be four distinct positive quantities in H.P. then show that $a+d>b+c.$ 

Solution : 

$\text{a},\text{b},\text{c},\text{d}$ are in H.P.

Then $\text{AM}>\text{H}.\text{M}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

For first three terms

$\therefore$ $\frac{a+c}{2}>b$

$\Rightarrow$ $a+c>2 b$ (1)

And for last three terms

$\frac{b+d}{2}>c$

$\Rightarrow$ $b+d>2 c$ (2)

From (1) and (2)

$a+c+b+d>2 b+2 c$

$\Rightarrow$ $a+d>b+c$

Q) Prove that ${{\left( \frac{a+b}{2} \right)}^{a+b}}>{{a}^{b}}{{b}^{a}},ab\in \mathbf{N}\And a\ne b$. 

Solution 

By just seeing the question we get the feel that it is kind of $\text{AM}>\text{GM}$ So, we get the idea from R.H.S.

a repeated b times$\And $ b repeated a times

Now applying $\text{AM}>\text{GM}$

$\frac{(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{a}+\ldots \mathrm{b} \text { times })+(\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{b}+\ldots \ldots . \mathrm{a} \text { times })}{\begin{array}{c}\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} \\ \left(\begin{array}{l}\text { since no. of terms }) \\ \text { are } \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}\end{array}\right.\end{array}}$$>[(\text { a.a.a...) (b..b..b. })]^{\frac{1}{a+b}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{ab+ab}{a+b}>{{\left( {{a}^{b}}{{b}^{a}} \right)}^{\frac{1}{a+b}}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{2ab}{a+b}>{{\left( {{a}^{b}}{{b}^{a}} \right)}^{\frac{1}{a+b}}}$ (i)

Now, we know that $\text{AM}>\text{HM}$

i.e. $\frac{a+b}{2}>\frac{2ab}{a+b}$ (ii)

So from (i) & (ii)

$\frac{a+b}{2}>\left(a^b b^a\right)^{\frac{1}{a+b}}$

$\Rightarrow$ $\left(\frac{a+b}{2}\right)^{a+b}>a^b b^a$
